planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before first fall frost and transplant in late summer/early fall; in zone 9 direct sow or transplant September–October for fall and winter harvest. Winterbor kale tolerates temperatures to 15°F and improves significantly in flavor after frost.

directions: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before fall transplanting or direct sow in late summer for fall production; Winterbor is a Dutch hybrid kale developed by Royal Sluis that became one of the most popular commercial and home garden kale varieties worldwide, valued for its combination of highly productive, tight, attractive blue-green curly leaves, exceptional cold hardiness, and distinctively superior flavor after frost. The tightly ruffled, heavily curled leaves are more tender and less tough than some older kale varieties, making Winterbor more pleasant to eat both raw in salads and cooked. The blue-green color is attractive and the upright habit (plants grow upward rather than sprawling) makes garden management easy. Winterbor is exceptionally cold-hardy — tolerating temperatures to 15°F without damage — and the cold-sweetening effect that makes kale improve after frost is particularly pronounced in Winterbor, making late-season harvested plants dramatically sweeter and more tender than those harvested before cold weather. In zone 9, Winterbor is primarily grown as a fall-through-winter crop (September–February) when temperatures allow proper kale production; it tolerates Gulf Coast mild winters better than more tender greens.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Winterbor kale variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Kale Production Guide

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 transplant February–March for spring harvest or August–September for fall. Packman is an early variety well-adapted to the shorter cool seasons available in zone 9's spring and fall broccoli windows.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 60–75°F; Packman broccoli is a 1982 All-America Selections-winning hybrid that remains one of the most widely grown garden broccoli varieties — an early-maturing variety (65–75 days from transplant) producing large, blue-green central heads with a domed, attractive appearance and good flavor. After the central head is harvested, Packman produces generous side shoots that continue through the season, extending harvest considerably beyond what determinate, single-head broccoli types provide. The early maturation is particularly valuable for zone 9 gardeners who need a broccoli that produces before summer heat terminates the season, and for northern gardeners who want to beat early fall frosts. Packman is dependable, widely available, and well-adapted to a range of growing conditions — making it an excellent beginner's broccoli and a reliable backbone variety for any broccoli production.
sources: All-America Selections — Packman broccoli AAS winner 1982
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Broccoli Production Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Vegetable Variety Trials

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F; plant 4–5 seeds per hill 1 inch deep, thin to 2 strongest plants when 3 inches tall. In zone 9 plant March–April for summer harvest; Halona's short season (73–80 days) makes it one of the better choices for zone 9's limited spring melon window before extreme summer heat.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F; Halona is a 2015 All-America Selections Gold Medal winner cantaloupe — one of the most highly awarded AAS winners in recent years — developed for exceptional eating quality combined with early maturation and disease resistance. Halona produces medium-sized (4–5 pound), deeply netted, tan-skinned fruits with dense, deep orange flesh and exceptional sweetness (Brix 14–17°, very high for an open-pollinated type) with complex, honey-like flavor that compares to the finest specialty melons. The fruit set is consistent and the plants show resistance to powdery mildew — a significant disease in humid climates. Halona's early maturation (73–80 days) and compact vine habit make it suitable for small gardens and shorter growing seasons; two plants produce more than enough for a family. The small seed cavity and thick, firm flesh give excellent eating quality with no watery texture. Halona is the recommended first melon for new growers due to its reliable production, disease resistance, and superior flavor — and one of the best choices for zone 9 spring production where a short fruiting window requires a reliable early variety.
sources: All-America Selections — Halona cantaloupe AAS Gold Medal winner 2015
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Halona variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cantaloupe Production Guide

common_name: Rocky Ford Cantaloupe (Colorado Classic Heirloom Cantaloupe)

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F; Rocky Ford reaches peak flavor in dry, hot summer conditions and performs best in climates with hot days, warm nights, and low humidity — it is excellent in the arid Southwest and Mountain West but somewhat prone to fungal diseases in humid Gulf Coast conditions.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F; harvest when stem slips easily from the vine ('slip' stage) and the blossom end is slightly soft. Rocky Ford is the historic heirloom cantaloupe variety that made the Arkansas River Valley around Rocky Ford, Colorado famous for melon production in the late 19th century — producing medium-sized, netted, round to slightly oval fruits with bright green skin under tan netting and pale green to orange flesh with exceptional sweetness and classic old-fashioned cantaloupe flavor. The variety was so famous that 'Rocky Ford' became synonymous with high-quality cantaloupe across America in the early 20th century, and special rail cars shipped Rocky Ford melons to Chicago, New York, and other major cities during the peak season. Rocky Ford performs best in dry climates with hot days and cool nights (like the high-altitude semi-arid conditions of Colorado) where low humidity reduces fungal disease and water-stressed plants concentrate sugars at maturity. In humid Gulf Coast conditions it requires careful disease management, but dedicated heirloom melon enthusiasts appreciate its distinctive flavor, which has a more complex, old-fashioned character than modern hybrids.
sources: Colorado State University Extension — Melon Production
Seed Savers Exchange — Rocky Ford cantaloupe variety history

common_name: Florida Giant Watermelon (Southern Heirloom Picnic Watermelon)

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May for June–August harvest. Florida Giant requires a long warm season (85–100 days) and ample space for the vigorous, sprawling vines — allow 6–10 feet diameter per plant. Very heat-tolerant and well-adapted to Gulf Coast summer conditions.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills after last frost when soil is above 70°F, planting 4–5 seeds per hill and thinning to 2 plants; harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit has dried and the ground spot has turned from white to cream-yellow. Florida Giant is a classic large-fruited Southern heirloom watermelon producing enormous fruits (30–60 pounds or more) with light green to dark green striped skin and deep red, sweet flesh — the quintessential 'picnic' or 'table' watermelon of the Gulf South where the long, hot summer provides adequate heat units for the 85–100 day maturation period. The very large fruits are challenging to transport single-handed but are impressive in the garden and at the table, providing abundant fresh fruit for summer gatherings. Florida Giant performs excellently under Gulf Coast hot humid summer conditions, showing reasonable tolerance of the fungal diseases (Fusarium wilt, anthracnose) common in humid climates, and is well-adapted to the sandy, fertile, moderately acid soils of Florida and the Gulf Coast region. The traditional size and flavor make it a regional staple and a nostalgic choice for gardeners who remember the giant watermelons of summer cookouts.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Watermelon Production Guide
